## **Coresets for Clustering with Missing Values**

## **Vladimir Braverman**

Johns Hopkins University `vova@cs.jhu.edu`

**Shaofeng H.-C. Jiang** Peking University `shaofeng.jiang@pku.edu.cn`

**Robert Krauthgamer** Weizmann Institute of Science `robert.krauthgamer@weizmann.ac.il`

**Xuan Wu** Johns Hopkins University `wu3412790@gmail.com`

## **Abstract**

We provide the first coreset for clustering points in R _[d]_ that have multiple missing values (coordinates). Previous coreset constructions only allow one missing coordinate. The challenge in this setting is that objective functions, like _k_ -MEANS, are evaluated only on the set of available (non-missing) coordinates, which varies across points. Recall that an _ϵ_ -coreset of a large dataset is a small proxy, usually a reweighted subset of points, that (1 + _ϵ_ )-approximates the clustering objective for every possible center set.

Our coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering have size ( _jk_ ) _[O]_[(min(] _[j,k]_[))] ( _ϵ[−]_[1] _d_ log _n_ )[2] , where _n_ is the number of data points, _d_ is the dimension and _j_ is the maximum number of missing coordinates for each data point. We further design an algorithm to construct these coresets in near-linear time, and consequently improve a recent quadratic-time PTAS for _k_ -MEANS with missing values [Eiben et al., SODA 2021] to near-linear time.

We validate our coreset construction, which is based on importance sampling and is easy to implement, on various real data sets. Our coreset exhibits a flexible tradeoff between coreset size and accuracy, and generally outperforms the uniformsampling baseline. Furthermore, it significantly speeds up a Lloyd’s-style heuristic for _k_ -MEANS with missing values.

## **1 Introduction**

We consider coresets and approximation algorithms for _k_ -clustering problems, particularly _k_ -MEANS[1] and more generally ( _k, z_ )-CLUSTERING (see Definition 2.1), for points in R _[d]_ with _missing values (coordinates)_ . The presence of missing values in data sets is a common phenomenon, and dealing with it is a fundamental challenge in data science. While data imputation is a very popular method for handling missing values, it often requires prior knowledge which might not be available, or statistical assumptions on the missing values that might be difficult to verify [All01, LR19]. In contrast, our worst-case approach does not requires any prior knowledge. Specifically, in our context of clustering, the distance dist( _x, c_ ) between a clustering center point _c_ and a data point _x_ is evaluated only on the available (i.e., non-missing) coordinates. Similar models that aim to minimize clustering costs using only the available coordinates have been proposed in previous work [HB01, Wag04, CCB16, WLH[+] 19], and some other relevant works were discussed in a survey [HC10].
Clustering under this distance function, which is evaluated only on the available coordinates, is a formidable computational challenge, because distances do not satisfy the triangle inequality, and

> 1In the usual _k_ -MEANS problem (without missing coordinates), the input is a data set _X ⊂_ R _d_ and the goal is to find a center set _C ⊂_ R _[d] , |C|_ = _k_ that minimizes the sum of squared distances from every _x ∈ X_ to _C_ .

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

therefore many classical and effective clustering algorithms, such as _k_ -MEANS++ [AV07], cannot be readily applied or even be defined properly. Despite the algorithmic interest in clustering with missing values, the problem is still not well understood and only a few results are known. In a pioneering work, Gao, Langberg and Schulman [GLS08] initiated the algorithmic study of the _k_ -CENTER problem with missing values. They took a geometric perspective and interpreted the _k_ -CENTER with missing values problem as an affine-subspace clustering problem, and followup work [GLS10, LS13] has subsequently improved and generalized their algorithm. Only very recently, approximation algorithms for objectives other than _k_ -CENTER, particularly _k_ -MEANS, were obtained for the limited case of at most one missing coordinate in each input point [MF19] or for constant number of missing coordinates [EFG[+] 21].

We focus on designing coresets for clustering with missing values. Roughly speaking, an _ϵ_ -coreset is a small proxy of the data set, such that the clustering objective is preserved within (1 _± ϵ_ ) factor for all center sets (see Definition 2.2 for formal definition). Efficient constructions of small _ϵ_ -coresets usually lead to efficient approximations schemes, since the input size is reduced to that of the coreset, see e.g. [HJLW18, FRS19, MF19]. Moreover, apart from speeding up approximation algorithms in the classical setting (offline computation), coresets can also be applied to design streaming [HM04, FS05, BFL[+] 17], distributed [BEL13, RPS15, BLK18], and dynamic algorithms [Cha09, HK20], which are effective methods/models for dealing with big data, and recently coresets were used even in neural networks [MOB[+] 20].

**Experiments.** We implement our algorithm and validate its performance on various real and synthetic data sets in Section 4. Our coreset exhibits flexible tradeoffs between coreset size and accuracy, and generally outperforms a uniform-sampling baseline and a baseline that is based on imputation, in both error rate and stability, especially when the coreset size is relatively small. In particular, on each data set, a coreset of moderate size 2000 (which is 0 _._ 5%-5% of the data sets) achieves low empirical error (5%-20%). We further demonstrate an application and use our coresets to accelerate a Lloyd’s-style heuristic adapted to the missing-values setting. The experiments suggest that running the heuristic on top of our coresets gives equally good solutions (error _<_ 1% relative to running on the original data set) but is much faster (speedup _>_ 5x).

## **1.2 Technical Overview**

Our coreset construction is based on the importance sampling framework introduced by Feldman and Langberg [FL11] and subsequently improved and generalized by [FSS20, BJKW21]. In the framework, one first computes an importance score _σx_ for every data point _x ∈ X_ , and then draws independent samples with probabilities proportional to these scores. When no values are missing, the importance scores can be computed easily, even for general metric spaces [VX12b, FSS20, BJKW21]. However, a significant challenge with missing values is that distances do not satisfy the triangle inequality, hence importance scores cannot be easily computed.

We overcome this hurdle using a method introduced by Varadarajan and Xiao [VX12a] for projective clustering (where the triangle inequality similarly does not hold). They reduce the importancescore computation to the construction of a coreset for _k_ -CENTER objective; this method is quite different from earlier approaches, e.g. [FL11, VX12b, FSS20, BJKW21], and yields a coreset for _k_ -MEANS whose size depends linearly on log _n_ and of course on the size of the _k_ -CENTER coreset. (Mathematically, this arises from the sum of all importance scores.) We make use of this reduction, and thus focus on constructing (efficiently) a small coreset for _k_ -CENTER with missing values.

An immediate difficulty is how to deal with the missing values. We show that it is possible to find a collection of subsets of coordinates _I_ (so each _I ∈I_ is a subset of [ _d_ ]), such that if we construct _k_ -CENTER coresets _SI_ on the data set “restricted” to each _I ∈I_ , then the union of these _SI_ ’s is a _k_ -CENTER coreset for the original data set with missing values. Crucially, we ensure that each “restricted” data set does not contain any missing value, so that it is possible to use a classical coreset construction for _k_ -CENTER. Finally, we show in a technical lemma how to find a collection as necessary of size _|I| ≤_ ( _jk_ ) _[O]_[(min] _[{][j,k][}]_[)] .

Since a “restricted” data set does not contain any missing values, we can use a classical _k_ -CENTER coreset construction, and a standard construction has size _O_ ( _kϵ[−][d]_ ) [AP02], which is known to be tight. We bypass this _ϵ[−][d]_ limitation by observing that actually _O_[˜] (1)-coreset for _k_ -CENTER suffices, even though the final coreset error is _ϵ_ . We observe that an _O_[˜] (1)-coreset can be constructed using a variant of Gonzalez’s algorithm [Gon85].

To implement Gonzalez’s algorithm, a key step is to find the _furthest_ neighbor of a given subset of at most _O_ ( _k_ ) points, and a naive implementation of this runs in linear time, which overall yields a quadratic-time coreset construction, because the aforementioned reduction of [VX12a] actually requires Θ( _n/k_ ) successive runs of Gonzalez’s algorithm. To resolve this issue, we propose a fully-dynamic implementation of Gonzalez’s algorithm so that a furthest-point query is answered in time poly( _k_ log _n_ ), and the point-set is updated between successive runs instead of constructed from scratch. Our dynamic algorithm is based on a random-projection method that was proposed for furthest-point queries in the streaming setting [Ind03]. Specifically, we project the (restricted) data

## **1.3 Additional Related Work**

Coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering have been studied extensively for two decades, and we only list a few notable results. The first strong coresets for Euclidean _k_ -MEANS and _k_ - MEDIAN were given in [HM04]. In the last decade, most work on coresets for clustering follows the importance sampling framework initiated in [LS10, FL11]. In Euclidean space, recent work showed that coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering can have size that is independent of the Euclidean dimension [FSS20, SW18, HV20]. Beyond Euclidean space, coresets of size independent of the data-set size were constructed also for many important metric spaces [HJLW18, BJKW21, CASS21]. A more comprehensive overview can be found in recent surveys [Phi17, Fel20].

Recently, attention was given also to non-traditional settings of coresets for clustering, including coresets for Gaussian mixture models (GMM) [LFKF17, FKW19]; simultaneous coresets for a large family of cost functions that include both _k_ -MEDIAN and _k_ -CENTER [BJKW19]; and coresets for clustering under fairness constraints [HJV19]. Also considered were settings that capture uncertainty, for example when each point is only known to lie in a line (i.e., clustering lines) [MF19], and when each point comes from a finite set (i.e., clustering point sets) [JTMF20].

As mentioned in Section 1, we use importance sampling method which is a well-known technique for constructing coresets [FL11, FSS20]. A key step is to compute for every data point _x ∈ X_ an importance score _σx ≥_ 0 that estimates its maximum relative contribution to any solution. The computation of _{σx}_ is standard in metric spaces, see e.g. [FSS20, BJKW21], but this is not applicable for us because distances with missing values do not satisfy the triangle inequality. Hence, we employ an alternative approach proposed by Varadarajan and Xiao [VX12a, Lemma 3.1], which reduces the computation of _{σx}_ to finding coresets for _k_ -CENTER. _k_ **-CENTER coreset for restricted data set via Gonzalez’s algorithm.** Finally, the _k_ -CENTER coreset for the restricted data set on each _I ∈I_ is constructed using an approximate version of Gonzalez’s algorithm [Gon85], where we first pick an arbitrary data point as the initial coreset, and in every iteration an _O_[˜] (1)-approximate furthest neighbor in the dataset is picked into the coreset, and˜ we do this for _k_ times. **Dynamic implementation of Gonzalez’s algorithm.** To make this _k_ -CENTER coreset construction dynamic, we adapt the random projection technique to Gonzalez’s algorithm. We project the (restricted) point set onto several random lines and use a one-dimensional data structure to construct _k_ -CENTER coreset for each of these (projected) one-dimensional data set. Note that the key step in Gonzalez’s algorithm is finding a furthest neighbor, and we can show that our projection method yields an _O_ ( _k[√]_ log _n_ )-approximation of the furthest neighbor with high probability.

## **5 Conclusion**

Our coreset construction builds upon the sensitivity-sampling method (cf. [FL11]). However, a central technical challenge is that the standard method to compute the sensitivity scores breaks, because distances between points with missing values do not satisfy the triangle inequality. We overcome this using another known method, of [VX12a], that requires a coreset for _k_ -CENTER. Our main innovation is a near-linear time algorithm that computes an _O_ (1)-approximate _k_ -CENTER coreset for points with missing values. To this end, we need the following key steps, which constitute our main technical contribution.

- We reduce the _k_ -CENTER coreset construction with missing values, to the construction of traditional _k_ -CENTER coresets (i.e., without missing values) on a series of instances. These instances are built by restricting data points with missing values to a carefully-chosen collection of subspaces. The guarantee needed from this collection is a certain combinatorial structure, and we indeed prove it exists.

- The method of Varadarajan and Xiao executes the _k_ -CENTER coreset algorithm many times, and overall takes quadratic time. To improve the running time, we design an efficient dynamic algorithm for the well-known Gonzales’ algorithm (which computes an _O_ (1)approximate _k_ -CENTER coreset). The main idea in this dynamic algorithm is to project the data points onto (data-oblivious) random 1D lines, and build on each line a dynamic data structure that supports furthest-neighbor queries (in 1D).
Finally, we implemented our algorithm and the experiments indicate that our algorithm is efficient and accurate enough to be potentially applicable in practice.

**Future directions.** As an immediate follow-up, one could try to improve our coreset size, e.g., removing the dependence in log _n_ . Our input can be viewed as axis-parallel affine-subspaces. Hence, another an interesting direction is to obtain coresets for the more general setting where the input consists of general affine-subspaces.

**Potential negative societal impacts.** Our paper focuses on computational issues (improving time and space) of known clustering tasks. Clustering methods in general have potential issues with fairness and privacy, which applies also to our work, but our research is not expected to introduce new negative societal impact beyond what is already known.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This introduction is highly effective, demonstrating both clear technical maturity and rhetorical precision required for a venue like NeurIPS. The authors successfully frame the "missing values" problem as a significant challenge—specifically by highlighting the loss of the triangle inequality—which justifies the necessity of the proposed coreset approach. The narrative flow is logical, transitioning seamlessly from the limitations of existing imputation-based methods to the theoretical contributions and the computational improvements offered by the new algorithm. Furthermore, the inclusion of an impossibility result (Theorem 1.2) significantly elevates the paper’s quality, as it demonstrates a deep understanding of the problem's complexity rather than just a heuristic improvement. The only potential risk is the reliance on the merge-and-reduce method for streaming applications, which, while standard, warrants a clear empirical discussion to ensure that the "theoretical" near-linear time remains efficient in practice for high-dimensional applications. Overall, the foundational clarity and the technical ambition of the problem definition signal a high-quality contribution.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a significant theoretical advance by providing the first coreset constructions for $k$-clustering with multiple missing coordinates, effectively bridging a notable gap in the literature where prior work was restricted to the single-missing-coordinate case. The contribution is technically robust, highlighted by the near-linear time construction, the establishment of a tight lower bound demonstrating that the exponential dependence on $\min(j,k)$ is inherent, and the practical improvement over the recent SODA 2021 PTAS. However, the most significant issue is the scalability of the coreset size, which grows exponentially with $\min(j,k)$ and polynomially squared in $d$ and $\epsilon^{-1}$; while the dependence on $d$ and $\epsilon$ is polynomial, the exponential bottleneck in $j$ and $k$, combined with the lack of the $\epsilon^{-1}$ dependence common in state-of-the-art standard coresets, severely limits practical applicability for problems with non-trivial numbers of clusters or dimensions. The feedback urges the authors to provide a more nuanced discussion regarding the regime of utility, explicitly quantifying the failure of the coreset for moderate $k$ or $j$, and to clarify the abstract's characterization of the size as a "low-degree polynomial," which may mislead readers into underestimating the exponential dependence on $j$ and $k$ that constrains the method's real-world utility.

comments.explanation: I checked the time complexity in Corollary 1.3 to ensure it correctly accounts for running the PTAS on the coreset. Since the coreset size is $m = (jk)^{O(\min\{j,k\})} (\epsilon^{-1} d \log n)^2$, executing the PTAS on the coreset introduces a $m^{O(1)} = \text{poly}(\log n)$ factor. The corollary absorbs this polylogarithmic factor into $d^{O(1)}$ (or suppresses it). While this is standard practice in parameterized complexity and theoretical CS to emphasize the independence from $n$ after coreset reduction, explicitly writing $2^{\text{poly}(jk/\epsilon)} \cdot \tilde{O}(d^{O(1)})$ would be more precise and prevent a careful reader from wondering about the lingering $\log n$ dependence in the second term.

comments.title: High-level sketch of dynamic Gonzalez algorithm
comments.quote: Our dynamic algorithm is based on a random-projection method that was proposed for furthest-point queries in the streaming setting [Ind03]. Specifically, we project the (restricted) data set onto several random directions, and on each projected (one-dimensional) data set we apply a data structure for intervals.
comments.explanation: The technical overview correctly identifies the high-level strategy (random projection to 1D followed by interval data structures) to enable dynamic furthest-point queries for Gonzalez's algorithm, which is crucial for avoiding the quadratic-time bottleneck. However, it omits key technical choices: how the projection dimension is determined, how 1D $k$-center coresets are maintained under point deletions/insertions, and how the results across dimensions are aggregated to guarantee the $O(1)$-coreset property in the original space. As this is a 1.2 overview section, these omissions are expected, but a reproducer would need to refer to the full version for the precise data structure updates, failure probability bounds, and query/insertion time analysis.

comments.title: Unexpected approximation factor O(k√log n) in dynamic Gonzalez's algorithm
comments.quote: Note that the key step in Gonzalez’s algorithm is finding a furthest neighbor, and we can show that our projection method yields an O(k√log n)-approximation of the furthest neighbor with high probability.
comments.explanation: Standard random projection techniques for Euclidean furthest-point queries typically yield an approximation factor of O(√(log n)) or O(√d), independent of the number of centers k. The explicit multiplicative factor of k is unexpected and would significantly degrade the coreset quality if k is large. It is unclear whether this factor is a typographical error (e.g., intended as O(√(k log n)) or simply O(√log n)), arises from an analysis of querying k centers in 1D, or requires specific justification in the reduction. Without clarification, the stated approximation guarantee for the dynamic construction is ambiguous.

comments.title: Logical overclaim regarding coreset error and algorithm performance
comments.quote: This 5%-20% error is likely to be enough for practical use, since practical algorithms for k-MEANS are approximation algorithms anyway.
comments.explanation: The authors justify their experimental error rates by noting that practical clustering algorithms are inherently approximation algorithms. However, an empirical error of 5%-20% measured on a random sample of centers does not directly translate to the approximation ratio or solution quality of an iterative algorithm like Lloyd's heuristic. A coreset's error bound describes how well it preserves the objective function across center sets, but it does not guarantee that minimizing the objective on the coreset will yield a nearby solution for the original data. The claim overstates the implications of the empirical results for algorithmic performance.

comments.title: Suboptimal complexity claim for 1D furthest neighbor query
comments.quote: For each one-dimensional line, we use a balanced search tree structure to support the point update and the furthest neighbor query. All operations can be done in O(k log m) time where m is the number of currently inserted elements.
comments.explanation: The passage states that finding the point furthest from a set of k centers in a 1D projection takes O(k log m) time using a balanced BST. While querying each of the k centers individually in a BST yields O(k log m), the 1D furthest-neighbor-from-a-set problem can be resolved in O(log m) time by maintaining the k induced intervals (Voronoi regions) or by binary searching the endpoints of the k-1 gaps. The authors do not explain the k factor, which makes the method slightly underspecified and suggests a possibly suboptimal implementation choice without justification.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in approximation guarantee (tilde vs non-tilde)
comments.quote: We observe that an O_[˜] (1)-coreset can be constructed using a variant of Gonzalez’s algorithm [Gon85]. ... Our main innovation is a near-linear time algorithm that computes an O(1)-approximate k-CENTER coreset for points with missing values.
comments.explanation: In Section 1.2, the authors refer to an '$\tilde{O}(1)$-coreset' constructed via a dynamic variant of Gonzalez's algorithm that relies on random projections. Dynamic or randomized furthest-neighbor structures typically incur logarithmic factors in their approximation guarantees or failure probabilities, which justifies the $\tilde{O}(1)$ notation. However, in the Conclusion, the authors state the innovation is an '$O(1)$-approximate k-CENTER coreset', dropping the tilde. While Gonzalez's algorithm inherently provides a factor of 2 ($O(1)$) approximation in the static metric setting, the dynamic projected version likely retains logarithmic factors in its quality or size. This inconsistency could mislead readers regarding the exact approximation quality of the dynamic construction, though it is likely a minor notational oversight rather than a substantive error.

comments.title: Insufficient justification in Lemma 3.3 proof sketch
comments.quote: Since I is a (j,k,d)-family and |Ix| ≥ d − j, by definition, there exists an I ⊆ I such that K ⊆ I ⊆ Ix.
comments.explanation: The proof sketch claims that a set $I \in \mathcal{I}$ exists such that $K \subseteq I \subseteq I_x$ directly by definition. However, Definition 3.3 only guarantees that for disjoint $J, K$, there exists $I$ with $K \subseteq I$ and $I \cap J = \emptyset$. It does not explicitly guarantee $I \subseteq I_x$. The proof implicitly assumes one can construct $J$ by taking the missing coordinates $[d] \setminus I_x$ (which has size at most $j$ since $x$ is a $j$-point) and extending it to a set of size $j$ disjoint from $K$. Because $I \cap J = \emptyset$, this would force $I \subseteq [d] \setminus ([d] \setminus I_x) = I_x$. This combinatorial extension step is non-trivial for a proof sketch and skips a crucial logical link. A careful reader would need to reconstruct this argument independently, making the derivation opaque without the explicit justification.

comments.title: Underspecified experimental method for Lloyd's adaptation
comments.quote: The algorithm is essentially the same as the original Lloyd’s algorithm, except that the distance as well as the optimal 1-mean for a cluster (which can be computed optimally in O(d|P|) for a cluster P [EFG+] 21), is computed differently.
comments.explanation: The description of the modified Lloyd's heuristic is vague. While it correctly notes that distance computation and the 1-mean update must be adapted for missing values (citing EFG+ 21 for the $O(d|P|)$ mean computation), stating they are 'computed differently' omits key algorithmic details. Important choices for reproducibility—such as the initialization method (e.g., k-means++ vs random), handling of empty clusters, stopping criteria, and the exact formula for the center when coordinates are missing for some points—are left implicit. Given that the paper claims significant experimental speedup and accuracy improvements based on this heuristic, providing explicit adaptation rules would strengthen the reproducibility of the results, even if some details are relegated to a citation.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a significant theoretical advance by providing the first coreset constructions for $k$-clustering with multiple missing coordinates, effectively bridging a notable gap in the literature where prior work was restricted to the single-missing-coordinate case. The contribution is technically robust, highlighted by the near-linear time construction, the establishment of a tight lower bound demonstrating that the exponential dependence on $\min(j,k)$ is inherent, and the practical improvement over the recent SODA 2021 PTAS. However, the most significant issue is the scalability of the coreset size, which grows exponentially with $\min(j,k)$ and polynomially squared in $d$ and $\epsilon^{-1}$; while the dependence on $d$ and $\epsilon$ is polynomial, the exponential bottleneck in $j$ and $k$, combined with the lack of the $\epsilon^{-1}$ dependence common in state-of-the-art standard coresets, severely limits practical applicability for problems with non-trivial numbers of clusters or dimensions. The feedback urges the authors to provide a more nuanced discussion regarding the regime of utility, explicitly quantifying the failure of the coreset for moderate $k$ or $j$, and to clarify the abstract's characterization of the size as a "low-degree polynomial," which may mislead readers into underestimating the exponential dependence on $j$ and $k$ that constrains the method's real-world utility.

The text does not explicitly state this condition or address the case where $d < j+k$. While the family construction in Lemma 3.4 likely handles small dimensions trivially, explicitly noting this assumption would prevent a careful reader from questioning the combinatorial step. progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and significant contribution to the theory of coresets for clustering with missing values, extending prior work that only handled a single missing coordinate to the general case of multiple missing coordinates per point. The exposition is clear and well-structured, with the abstract and introduction effectively motivating the problem and summarizing the main results, including both upper and lower bounds. A notable strength is the near-linear time construction and the validation on real data, which bridges theory and practice. However, the paper's most significant issue lies in the exponential dependence on \(\min(j,k)\) in the coreset size (even if shown to be necessary in a certain regime). This dependence may limit practical applicability when both \(j\) and \(k\) are moderately large, and the paper does not fully discuss potential relaxations or alternative approaches to mitigate this. Additionally, while the abstract mentions a “flexible tradeoff between coreset size and accuracy,” the provided excerpt does not elaborate on how this tradeoff is achieved or whether it can be tuned by the user. Overall, the paper is of high quality and clarity, but the scalability to large \(j\) and \(k\) remains a concern that warrants further discussion or experimental exploration in the full version.

comments.title: Missing superscript in abstract formula
comments.quote: Our coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering have size ( _jk_ ) _[O]_[(min(] _[j,k]_[))] ( _ϵ[−]_[1] _d_ log _n_ )[2]
comments.explanation: The notation `ϵ[−][1]` appears in the abstract. In context, this is clearly meant to be `ϵ^{-1}` (epsilon to the power -1). The surrounding brackets and the squared term suggest an OCR misread of a superscript. The correct intended formula should be (jk)^{O(min(j,k))} (ϵ^{-1} d log n)^2, which matches the form used later in Theorem 1.1. This is a rendering artifact, but it makes the expression hard to parse. Since the passage is from the abstract and the same expression appears correctly in Theorem 1.1 later, the issue is minor.

comments.title: Inconsistent parenthesization in Theorem 1.1 size expression
comments.explanation: The LaTeX/OCR output writes `ϵ[−][1]` instead of `ϵ^{-1}`. The square brackets likely come from OCR misinterpreting curly braces in a superscript. The intended expression is clearly `ϵ^{-1}`, as used later in the paper (e.g., the more formal Theorem 3.1 has `ϵ^{-2}`). This is a consistent OCR artifact across the abstract and theorem statement. It does not change the mathematical meaning, but it is a minor formatting issue that could confuse a reader not familiar with the paper's notation.

comments.title: Potential missing factor in Theorem 1.2 lower bound statement
comments.quote: then f ( _j, k_ ) _can not be as small as_ 2 _[o]_[(min(] _[j,k]_[))]
comments.explanation: The statement says f(j,k) cannot be as small as 2^{o(min(j,k))}. This is a typical lower bound phrasing. However, the claim seems to imply that f(j,k) must be at least 2^{Ω(min(j,k))}. This is plausible, but the phrasing 'cannot be as small as 2^{o(min(j,k))}' is equivalent to saying it is not o(2^{min(j,k)})? Actually, 2^{o(min(j,k))} is a slower-growing function than any exponential with constant base. The statement means that the exponent must be linear in min(j,k). The phrasing is correct but subtle: 'cannot be as small as 2^{o(min(j,k))}' means the exponent cannot be sublinear; so the exponent must be Ω(min(j,k)). A careful reader might momentarily think it says the size must be at least 2^{o(min(j,k))} (which would be very weak), but reading the whole sentence clarifies that it is a lower bound asserting exponential dependence. This is a minor clarity point.

comments.title: Potential mismatch between abstract and Theorem 3.1 for (k,z)-clustering size
comments.quote: Our coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering have size ( _jk_ ) _[O]_[(min(] _[j,k]_[))] ( _ϵ[−]_[1] _d_ log _n_ )[2]
comments.explanation: The abstract states the coreset size for k-MEANS and k-MEDIAN as (jk)^{O(min(j,k))} (ϵ^{-1} d log n)^2. However, Theorem 3.1 (which is the formal theorem) gives a more complex expression: Õ( z^z · (j^j + j^k k^k)/(j^k) · ... ) for (k,z)-clustering. When z=2 (k-MEANS) and z=1 (k-MEDIAN), the abstract's formula is a simplification. The abstract uses ϵ^{-1} while Theorem 3.1 uses ϵ^{-2}. This is a genuine discrepancy: the abstract's factor (ϵ^{-1} d log n)^2 is actually ϵ^{-2} (d log n)^2, which matches Theorem 3.1's ϵ^{-2}. So the abstract's notation (ϵ[−][1]) is just ϵ^{-1}, and squaring it gives ϵ^{-2}. So no actual numerical mismatch. The abstract's expression is consistent once the OCR is resolved. This is fine.

comments.title: Misplaced footnote marker in introduction
comments.quote: particularly _k_ -MEANS[1] and more generally ( _k, z_ )-CLUSTERING (see Definition 2.1), for points in R _[d]_ with _missing values (coordinates)_ . The presence of missing values...
comments.explanation: The footnote marker `[1]` appears immediately after 'k-MEANS' and the footnote at the bottom of the page defines standard k-MEANS. However, this footnote is placed within the running text, not at the end of the sentence. The placement is a bit awkward because it interrupts the flow, but it is standard for a definitional footnote. The authors likely intended to footnote the first use of 'k-MEANS' to clarify the non-missing-values version. This is a minor formatting choice, not an error.

comments.title: OCR artifact in Theorem 1.2: R _[d]_ ?
comments.quote: Consider the k -MEANS _with missing values problem in_ R _[d]_ ? _[where each point can]
comments.explanation: The text reads 'R _[d]_ ?' which is likely an OCR artifact. In the earlier abstract and introduction, the notation used is 'R _[d]_ ' or 'R _[d]_ ?' where the '?' is a placeholder for missing values. In Theorem 1.2, the '?' appears after the bracket, possibly as a misread of a superscript or special character. The intended notation is probably R^d (Euclidean space) or (R ∪ {?})^d as defined in Section 2. Given the context, this is a trivial OCR glitch that does not affect understanding.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in the reduction from Varadarajan-Xiao
comments.quote: We overcome this hurdle using a method introduced by Varadarajan and Xiao [VX12a, Lemma 3.1], which reduces the importancescore computation to the construction of a coreset for k -CENTER objective; this method is quite different from earlier approaches, e.g. [FL11, VX12b, FSS20, BJKW21], and yields a coreset for k -MEANS whose size depends linearly on log n and of course on the size of the k -CENTER coreset. (Mathematically, this arises from the sum of all importance scores.)
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the reduction yields a coreset whose size depends linearly on log n and on the k-CENTER coreset size. However, they do not explain how this reduction works or provide a citation to the specific lemma. The text says 'Mathematically, this arises from the sum of all importance scores.' This is somewhat vague. But this is an overview section, so it is acceptable to be high-level. The full details are presumably in the full paper. This is not an error, just a brief description.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Õ (1)-coreset' claim in technical overview
comments.quote: We observe that an Õ (1)-coreset can be constructed using a variant of Gonzalez's algorithm [Gon85].
comments.explanation: The authors claim that an Õ(1)-coreset for k-CENTER can be constructed using Gonzalez's algorithm, but they do not specify what Õ(1) means in this context. Usually, Gonzalez's algorithm gives a 2-approximation for k-CENTER, but here they are building a coreset, not an approximation. The phrase 'Õ(1)-coreset' likely means a constant-factor coreset (i.e., an α-coreset with α = O(1) up to polylog factors). This is plausible, but the justification is not given in this excerpt. The later Lemma 3.2 claims an O(k√d log q)-coreset, which is stronger. The overview says 'Õ(1)' but the actual result has factors of k and √d. This inconsistency is resolved by reading Lemma 3.2: the final bound for the dynamic algorithm is O(k√d log q). So the overview's 'Õ(1)' is a simplification. This could be misleading if a reader expects a truly constant factor independent of k and d. However, in context, the overview is informal. I'll flag this as a minor oversimplification.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of I_x
comments.quote: For x ∈ R^d_?, we denote the set of coordinates that are not missing by I_x := {i : x_i =?}.
comments.explanation: The text defines I_x as the set of coordinates that are not missing, but the condition given is x_i = ?, which indicates missing coordinates. This is a direct contradiction: if x_i = ?, the coordinate is missing, not present. The correct notation should be I_x := {i : x_i ≠ ?} (or a similar condition). The subsequent usage of I_x in the definition of j-points (|I_x| ≥ d−j) and in I-induced distances (I ⊆ I_p ∩ I_q) confirms that I_x is intended to be the set of non-missing coordinates, as in the earlier summary. The error could be an OCR misreading (e.g., the negation symbol was dropped), but as presented it is a real definition mistake that would confuse readers.

comments.title: Inconsistent runtime bound in Corollary 1.3
comments.explanation: The corollary claims that after constructing the coreset (size m = (jk)^{O(min{j,k})} · (ϵ^{-1} d log n)^2) in near-linear time, running the PTAS from [EFG+21] on the coreset yields total time dominated by the coreset construction plus an exponential term that depends only on d^{O(1)}. However, the PTAS is described earlier as having time O(2^{poly(jk/ϵ)} · n^2 d). Applied to a coreset of size m, its runtime would be O(2^{poly(jk/ϵ)} · m^2 d), which introduces additional factors of (jk)^{2·O(min{j,k})}, ϵ^{-4}, d^3, and (log n)^4 — not simply d^{O(1)}. The stated bound therefore omits the coreset-size dependence in the exponential term and is not justified by the information given. This appears to be an oversight; the total runtime would still be near-linear in n for constant j,k, but the explicit expression is inaccurate.

comments.title: Factor mismatch in Lemma 3.3 proof sketch
comments.quote: so |xti − c[i] ti[|][≥] ~~√~~ r d . . . As Y_I is an α-coreset for X|_I, we know that there exists y ∈ Y_I^{-1} such that . . .
comments.explanation: The proof attempts to show that ∪_I Y_I^{-1} is an α√d-coreset. From the chain: dist(x,C) ≥ r ⇒ dist_I(x,C) ≥ r/d (since max coordinate distance ≥ √(r/d) and that coordinate is in I). Using the α-coreset property on X|_I, we would get dist_I(y,C) ≥ (r/d)/α. Since dist(y,C) ≥ dist_I(y,C), we obtain dist(y,C) ≥ r/(α d). This gives an α d-coreset, not α√d. The missing step that would introduce a √d factor is unclear. progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and significant contribution to the theory of coresets for clustering with missing values, extending prior work that only handled a single missing coordinate to the general case of multiple missing coordinates per point. The exposition is clear and well-structured, with the abstract and introduction effectively motivating the problem and summarizing the main results, including both upper and lower bounds. A notable strength is the near-linear time construction and the validation on real data, which bridges theory and practice. However, the paper's most significant issue lies in the exponential dependence on \(\min(j,k)\) in the coreset size (even if shown to be necessary in a certain regime). This dependence may limit practical applicability when both \(j\) and \(k\) are moderately large, and the paper does not fully discuss potential relaxations or alternative approaches to mitigate this. Additionally, while the abstract mentions a “flexible tradeoff between coreset size and accuracy,” the provided excerpt does not elaborate on how this tradeoff is achieved or whether it can be tuned by the user. Overall, the paper is of high quality and clarity, but the scalability to large \(j\) and \(k\) remains a concern that warrants further discussion or experimental exploration in the full version.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous advancement in the algorithmic study of clustering with missing values, addressing a significant gap between previous work that was limited to a single missing coordinate and the broader, more complex problem of handling arbitrary missing data. The introduction is clear and effective, articulating the computational difficulties posed by the violation of the triangle inequality in this specific distance metric and correctly identifying the coreset abstraction as a suitable tool for scaling the problem. The main theoretical contribution—a near-linear time construction of coresets for $k$-Means (and $k$-MEDIAN) with $j$ missing coordinates—is substantial; it improves upon quadratic-time PTAS results and offers a tractable method for speeding up Lloyd-style heuristics in high-dimensional settings. A key trade-off of this construction is its coreset size, scaling as $(jk)^{O(\min(j,k))}$, a dependence that the authors justify as optimal through a matching hardness result, though this factor may present practical limitations for inputs where both $j$ and $k$ are large.

comments.title: Incorrect Definition of (k, z)-Clustering Objective
comments.quote: Definition 2.1 ((k, z)-CLUSTERING). ... let dist(x, C) := \sum_{z=1}^k dist(x, c_z)^z
comments.explanation: The formula in Definition 2.1 uses $z$ both as the summation index and as the problem parameter that determines the exponent. The text explicitly states that this problem is called 'k-MEDIAN when z=1 and k-MEANS when z=2'. However, the standard definition of k-MEDIAN is minimizing the sum of distances to the *nearest* center ($\min_{c \in C} dist(x, c)$), not the sum over *all* centers. Similarly, k-MEANS uses the sum of squared distances to the nearest center. The given formula $\sum_{z=1}^k dist(x, c_z)^z$ describes a different objective (sum of powers of distances to all centers), which contradicts the textual description and standard terminology. This is a fundamental mathematical error in the definition provided in the passage.

comments.title: Flawed Logic in Proof Sketch of Lemma 3.3
comments.quote: As Y_I is an \alpha-coreset for X|I, we know that there exists y \in Y_I such that dist(x|I, Y_I) <= \alpha \sqrt{r}
comments.explanation: The proof sketch claims that for a generic $\alpha$-coreset $Y_I$, there exists a specific point $y$ in the coreset that is close to an arbitrary data point $x$ in the restricted space ($dist(x|I, Y_I) \le \alpha \sqrt{r}$). However, a standard coreset guarantees an approximation of the *objective value* (the sum of distances), not the distance from an arbitrary query point to the coreset. This property (that the coreset contains a point near $x$) is non-trivial and generally false for $k$-CENTER coresets (e.g., sets of farthest points). The authors' justification for this step appears to be incorrect or relies on an unstated specific property of their construction not captured by the standard definition of an $\alpha$-coreset.

comments.title: Inappropriate reference to exponential-time algorithms
comments.quote: enumeration procedures that require \Theta(\exp(poly(\epsilon^{-1} jk))) time.
comments.explanation: The paragraph immediately preceding this sentence discusses the Lloyd's heuristic for speedup evaluation, which runs in approximately 22 seconds. The mention of 'enumeration procedures' requiring exponential time is nonsensical in this context. It appears to be a fragment or a copy-paste error from a theoretical comparison section, potentially intended to contrast the authors' algorithm with the exponential-time brute force or enumeration baseline mentioned earlier in the introduction. It confuses the narrative and misleads the reader into thinking the Lloyd's heuristic (the subject of the experiment) is exponential.
